messages.subject: [R-sig-teaching] R-sig-teaching mailing list
messages.body_plain:
Discussions held on the Isolated Statistics mailing list about the
use R in teaching introductory statistics courses led to the creation
of a new R SIG: R-sig-teaching.

The SIG description:
Special Interest Group (SIG) on teaching statistics with R. The
primary purpose of the group is to provide a forum where instructors
using R in their statistics courses can share ideas, teaching
materials, and experiences.  One particular focus of the SIG is to
provide helpful support to instructors new to R who are teaching
introductory statistics courses populated with students with little
experience in statistics, statistical software, and command line
interfaces.

Administrators:
               Albyn Jones <jones at reed.edu>
               Bret Larget <brlarget at wisc.edu>


Posting to the list is restricted to subscribers.  To post to the
list, send the email to r-sig-teaching at r-project.org.

To get help about using it, send a message with just the word `help'
as subject or in the body, to:

    r-sig-teaching-request at hypatia.math.ethz.ch

The easiest way to subscribe is to go to the web interface at
http://www.r-project.org/, and follow the link to Mailing Lists.

Albyn

messages.subject: [R-sig-teaching] resources
messages.body_plain:
This link

     http://www.statland.org/R/WhyR.htm

goes to a page that lists some of the advantages and disadvantages of
using R in an introductroy course.  From there you can link to the 11
handouts I used for a course I have taught online.  These might need
to be localized to your situation.  R was not the main software for
the course but an option for those planning to take further coures
where R WAS the official software.  So, I do not really know if anyone
ever used these pages.  If you find any errors or gltches, please let
me know.

messages.subject: [R-sig-teaching] query about how to organize an intro to R
messages.subject_clean: query about how to organize an intro to R
messages.body_plain:
I'm planning to utilize R for my intermediate stats
course in the spring.  I'd be most interested in
hearing summaries and/or receiving materials that
folks have used to introduce students to R, and guided
them through the mechanics of using the command
language, running scripts, etc.

Thanks in advance for any examples or suggestions.

Nick Horton
Department of Mathematics and Statistics
Smith College

messages.subject: [R-sig-teaching] R in intro stats
messages.body_plain:
I'm glad to see this sig list up and running.

I'm planning to use R for my *intro* stats class in the Winter. This is 
an experiment,
as our intro stats classes all use SPSS. So I'm using R for this one 
section (out of about 8 sections
a year).  I want to see (1) how easy/hard it is for
students to work in a command line environment and (2) whether there is some
pedagogical advantage to working with command lines rather than just 
point-and-click.

We've taken our GUI-based SPSS lab manual and rewritten it for R. If 
anyone would like to see it I can make it available. I'll probably need 
to supplement it with some very basic intro tutorials or
worksheets giving basic introduction. I do not intend to have these 
students (mostly poli sci,
econ and bio) writing scripts. But I do intend to use R lots in-class 
simulations and
presentations. If anyone has used R in the intro class I'd be interested 
in hearing your experiences.

Bob Dobrow
Carleton College

messages.subject: [R-sig-teaching] R in intro stats
messages.body_plain:
I may state the obvious, but just in case it ain't.. :

We have been using R for intro stats courses here at ETH only
for engineer- or math- like students (and continue to do so),
but not for e.g., biology or environmental science students.

However, starting this fall, we (well, most of our faculty) are
using or plan to use  Professor John Fox'  "RCmdr" ('R
Commander')  instead of the GUI stats package ('SyStat' I believe). 
One nice goal of the RCmdr is to give students a simple GUI but
simultaneously expose them to the language (which is visibly
produced by GUI clicking) such that they learn part of it "by
the way", and are lured into using it for things that go beyond
the GUI. 

I could also envisage to use the R Cmdr for the first half of
class and then to switch almost completely to "command line"
i.e., rather an R-aware smart editor such as Tinn-R, Emacs,
WinEdt, Kate, or the one that comes with R for MacOS X.

Martin Maechler, 
ETH Zurich

messages.subject: [R-sig-teaching] R in intro stats
messages.body_plain:
I have been using R (and Splus before that, and old S before that) in
my intro course for a long time.  Most of the students in the course
have never used a command line interface before.  They come from many
disciplines (Bio, Econ, Soc, Poli Sci, History, etc...). 

I see advantages and disadvantages: you certainly have to spend more
time on learning the software.  The simple fact that spelling and
capitalization (X vs x) cause trouble takes some time.  The payoff I
see is substantial; the flexibility of the graphics facilities are a
revelation for students who have used other statistical software.
Some students get interested in R as a language.  The ease of doing
simulations has great value.  The price is right: many students
install R on their own personal machines, and some even continue to
use it after the course ends.

I use R for in-class demos, as well as having a 1 hour lab session every 
week.  I have written online lab notes for each lab meeting, though I am
constantly rethinking and revising them.  

albyn
